speaker: interviewer
text: And rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape number four for Abe Danko, August 6, 1995. Yeah, so you were telling us about eating, about the food.

speaker: interviewee
text: So he told us to come 12 o'clock in the night. And he prepare potatoes, [NON-ENGLISH], and [NON-ENGLISH]. That's a soup. And the soup was the [NON-ENGLISH], this one. We ate. We was maybe about 80 or 90 people. And we pay him $50. Because we need-- we couldn't eat more. Because we need now a smuggler, to smuggle us through the border. And the smuggler wants $50. So we go ahead. And we smuggle-- 1 o'clock supposed to be the smuggling. And we pass the border. And we walked till a train. And we're already on the German border.

speaker: interviewer
text: So one guy called [NON-ENGLISH]. And he doesn't say nothing. And he knocks on our door, to a-- in a house. And he say he's hungry. About 1 o'clock, 1:30 maybe, he say, you know, go over there to the [NON-ENGLISH]. You know what [NON-ENGLISH] mean? The head of the city, the town. He'll come help you. I'm a poor guy. What do you want from me? He said, where he lives? He shows him where he lives. And he goes there, then knocks over there the door. And the guys open up. And he said to him, hey, [NON-ENGLISH], I'm hungry. I'm a Jew. And he took him in. He makes a light. He start writing him down. And he calls him up. And next to three or four store-- houses for the father. And this guy comes back with such a salami, with a whole bread. And what I going to tell you? The whole kibbutz wants to go.

speaker: interviewee
text: But 2:30 is supposed to come the train. So we sent delegations. They're not going to carry so much. And they brought the food. We got food. And the train comes. And we come up. And we go to Munchen.

speaker: interviewer
text: You went to where?

speaker: interviewee
text: We come to Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, to Munich.

speaker: interviewee
text: Munich.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: In German it's Munchen. We come to Munich. And they put us in, in the Hamburg [NON-ENGLISH]. This was a local-- this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: A local what?

speaker: interviewee
text: A local-- A building what is belong to the JCC, or this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh, a federation building.

speaker: interviewee
text: A federation building, yeah. And they come. And they start dividing the people. And they say, 100 goes to Feldafing. 100 goes to Fernwald. 100 goes to Landsberg.

speaker: interviewer
text: Alright. And what camp were you assigned to?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fernwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can you spell that for me?

speaker: interviewee
text: Fernwald.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewee
text: F-O- two points on top R-N-V- no, W-A-L-D.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. So that was the DP camp that you were assigned to?

speaker: interviewee
text: The DP camp, right.

speaker: interviewer
text: Can I stop you, just for a minute?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because what you didn't talk about, when you were talking about the war, was how you got separated from your family, and how you found out that they had been killed. Can you talk about that a little bit?

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know how they got killed.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I didn't know.

speaker: interviewer
text: You still don't know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, because I was told by people thinking that they took a-- my father was a tailor. And when he make a lot of money, and he tried to escape. And they shoot him on the wire, when he wants to cross the--

speaker: interviewer
text: Was that out of Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, in Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: In Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewee
text: In Czestochowa.

speaker: interviewer
text: So as far as you know, they were all killed in Czestochowa?

speaker: interviewee
text: All. All.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Mother with the small brother-- they were sent to Auschwitz.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because she doesn't want to separate with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because they want to separate the children. And he was then 9 years old, or 10 years old. So the want to separate. She don't want it. She goes with him.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the other boys?

speaker: interviewee
text: The other boys-- he was married already. The older brother was married. And his wife was pregnant. So he was going with his wife.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did they go? Do you know?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No. And the other boy what he was three years younger than me-- he was living until '45. And he was sent to [NON-ENGLISH]. And by the last minute, he disappear, and is killed. But the people who was with him said that they worked with him all the time. And that's what they-- now where we are?

speaker: interviewer
text: We are in the DP camp.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, when they come the DP camp, it was all nations-- Russian, Poles, Belgian, French. All nations was. So when we come in, you register. Every Jew says Palestine, Palestine, Palestine.

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that what you said?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: What did you say?

speaker: interviewee
text: America. You know why I said America?

speaker: interviewer
text: Why?

speaker: interviewee
text: Because I didn't believe in this. And I didn't believe in this. But at least in America I know I got two uncles here.

speaker: interviewer
text: And had you already met your wife at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. No.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you registered for America.

speaker: interviewee
text: America. And I was in kibbutz. And they tell me a story that's I must go tomorrow work. So they gave me a choice-- to go work as a policeman, or to go work in a hospital, or to be like a janitor. I said I'm going to go in hospital.

speaker: interviewer
text: To work?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. And other-- you help out, taking out from the operation room the people, and put them in the room. Bringing that operation in the room. Stay by the door, letting visitors go in. And you must have a pass to go in. It pass by a month or two, I get a check from the German government. I get a check-- I don't know, 90 marks or something. It doesn't matter. But the kibbutz takes away anyway from me. And I start playing soccer over there.

speaker: interviewer
text: And how did you meet your wife?

speaker: interviewee
text: My wife I met in a dance. I met her has a cousin. And we dance. Every night was dancing or theater. So I met her. That's when she took me home. And I saw my wife. And I come tomorrow. The cousin was not there. And she was in. So we talk, and talk till-- I saw her. And four weeks later, we was married. And we clean up the camp-- no other the nation. Just Jews. And I get out from the hospital. I get to be a policeman. I get to be a policeman. But I am not a businessman. There was such big [NON-ENGLISH], such a bad thing doing, that nobody could imagine what-- what-- what people can do.

speaker: interviewer
text: I was a policeman. And I got married maybe, what, two months, or three months. And I was staying on-- on my post-- let's see, by the magazine what food. So you are policeman say, oy, what you doing here? You just married. Go home. You got a young wife. I going to stay for you. Thank you. No? You know what they're doing? They took a truck. And they go into the magazine. And they took out all the cigarettes, all the chocolate what the UNRRA brought. And they sold it on the black market. Tomorrow they say, hey, you was staying on the post. What happened? I tell the truth. This guy come to me. He said, you just married, no? And I say, yeah. He say, go home. I going to stay for you. He took over. But he was from the clique from the other guy. So they wiped it up. And I couldn't make a living. So I got into the office, and I say, whatever immigration is, I want to go. There was an immigration to Norway. I say, I'll go. I already got my daughter. So Celia was born in the camp?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah. She was six month out already. We come to Norway-- very, very nice country. Such a polite people I never saw my life. They try to help you, with heart, whatever they can. I give you an example. If you sitting and working-- there's 10 people sitting and working, and a stranger walked in, and asked for something, everybody stopped, and listened. They'll say, hey, that man is a newcomer. He doesn't know. Go help him out. Go with him. It doesn't matter you lose your job. That's fine. You go ahead and help him. Don't worry. If it's going to rain, a package lays on the floor. This man going to take a package and put it behind, where the rain doesn't make wet. They never knew to lock a door-- to lock a door on a lock. But my wife-- she said to me, you know what, your daughter going to marry a [NON-ENGLISH].

speaker: interviewer
text: That's what she said to me. I said, why? Because that comes with Yom Kippur. There was no Jews. If I would go to the synagogue, I was the 10th men. I took three buses to go to the synagogue. And if I wouldn't come to synagogue, there would be no [NON-ENGLISH] be no daven in Yom Kippur. And the propaganda was big. So I pack everything, I go to Israel. And by this time, you had Henry also?

speaker: interviewee
text: No, Henry was born in Norway.

speaker: interviewer
text: Right, right. So when you left Norway, you left with your wife and two children?

speaker: interviewee
text: Right.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I go to Israel. And I come to Israel. I saw it is worse. Pure suffering. Is no-- I don't have any idea how a person can suffer so much. I'm going back to the council. And I'm telling him that I want to go back to Norway. He say, you go ahead.

speaker: interviewer
text: I say, no. I cannot. You give me the OK. He say, you've got a passport. Why you worry? I say, you find it out if I can go back. OK, tomorrow. I get it. Leave your passport. Tomorrow I come. And he said, no, you cannot go back. You sign it that you don't want to return anymore.

speaker: interviewee
text: Because we spent money for you, to bring it to you. And now you deserted. You want to run away. I wrote a letter to the king. And the king answer me the letter. He said, I know what you wrote. Because I wrote to him that even God forgive somebody make a mistake. And I made a mistake when I left. Please forgive me and let me go back. He said, if you wanted so much go back, leave your wife and children over there. Come back alone. And if you can show that you can make a living, and bring up your children, and with your wife, you're very welcome.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we started looking at this letter. And we remind ourself that I got here an uncle. And he left, in '39, a wife with three children in Poland. He never saw the wife and three children anymore. So we decided no, we're not going to go to Norway. We're going to register to America. So when did you come here?

speaker: interviewee
text: After 10 years to be in Israel.

speaker: interviewer
text: Oh. So '58?

speaker: interviewee
text: In '58 I come here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Did you come straight to Chicago?

speaker: interviewee
text: No. I come to Cleveland. Because Cleveland send me the-- the affidavit. That's why I come to--

speaker: interviewer
text: Is that where the uncle was?

speaker: interviewee
text: Her uncle, right, was in this one.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I got two uncles here, but they're both dead already. But she got an uncle in Cleveland. And he send her this one. And we come to Cleveland. And in Cleveland, there was not much to do. So I got a cousin. He was a baker.

speaker: interviewer
text: And he said to me, you know what? I'm going to give you to a baker, to be a sweet baker, you know, for sweet. And I'm a bread baker. So after them, we can open a business together. OK. Meanwhile, Chicago called me. And they said, what do you do? We go over there. And I told him what is going on. He say, you stupid. I got here black guys are paid more than $50 a week. Wait a minute. You got people who are paid more $50 a week? What I'm doing here? I figured this way about me. I said, I'm going to come tomorrow. And I don't say nothing. I just grab the train and come here. And you left your wife and the kids in Cleveland?

speaker: interviewee
text: In Cleveland. I come here. I found out that he wouldn't hire me. If I gave him $1,000 a day, he wouldn't hire me. He said, this is not a job for you. I just got the people. Because I got the black people. And he got a chemical-- leather cleaning, you know, with all the chemical.

speaker: interviewer
text: He said, this is not for a Jewish boy, or something. And I call up and say, I'm coming back. He said, don't come back. Because you lost your job here too. You picked up yourself, and you're going away. Don't come here. Where I got to go? Now not here, not here. So I have here the cousin, what I told. We was in Poland together. And he said, OK, let's find a job for you here. So they found a job for me Hart Schaffner Marx, for me and for my wife. But they're paying $1 and a quarter. And my wife got $0.90 an hour.

speaker: interviewee
text: You cannot make a living. I pay $30-- $125 a month rent. And it was $6 cost me the transportation. Because $0.25, $0.50 a week, $1 for both, because a quarter a ride, so $1 for both. It's $6 transportation. The gas, and electric, and telephone is another $5 a week. I cannot make a living here.

speaker: interviewer
text: But did you work at Hart Schaffner Marx?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, but while you're working there, you're thinking you can't make a living.

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I talk to my cousin. And he said to me, you know what, I'm going to look for a job for you, a better one. But meanwhile, don't lose the job, this one. And he found a job for me in a book bindery. And he-- tell him story, story, story. He say, you know what, bring him up. And they brought me up.

speaker: interviewer
text: And the guy look at me and said, did you ever work in a bindery? I said, to tell you the truth, I never saw a binder in my life. He say, you good for me. Because all the guys coming in say yeah, yeah, yeah, yeah. When they give them something, they don't know nothing.

speaker: interviewee
text: At least you honest. You said this-- I never saw a bindery-- right. He said, listen. I got here a guy. He's electrician. He helps me out every evening. I need a guy of his place. Because he will not work for me. You come after the job. Sit with him. I going to pay you $1 and a quarter for sitting. Catch the job, and you're going to have a good job.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, I come 5:00 to him. 4:30, I quit over there. Until I come, it's 5:00. I still until 9:00. I got $5, for sitting like I'm sitting with you. And I sit oh, about two weeks. And the guy said to me, are you want to try it? Is that OK? I said, no. He said, I don't want to work. I touched it. I ruined it. OK, it's too early. Sit. Another pass by two weeks. He say, you want to try it? OK. I try it. It's good. OK, keep going. And he sit down here. And I was sorry all my life. This guy takes money now, and I'm working. Before, he work and I took the money. And one day he said to me, you know what, tell John I'm not coming anymore. You are ready good. I said, no, you tell him. OK, I'm going to come tomorrow and tell him. He come tomorrow, and tells him the story. He said, OK, you can quit your job. I said, for how much? And he said, what do you mean for how much? $1 and a quarter. Why should I change my job? Oh, you can have a lot of overtime. Are you paying for overtime? No, we pay straight. But you're going to have a lot of hours. So you're going to have a lot of money. I didn't want this. What do you want? Not for $1 and a quarter. OK, I give you $1.50. OK.

speaker: interviewee
text: I work about three years. He told me this-- he told me $2.30 is the highest wages what they paying. And for $2.30 I couldn't even make a living. So I said to my uncle, you know what, you call up tomorrow, 12 o'clock. We have lunch. And we playing cards in the lunch. [PAUSES FOR 4 SECONDS]

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: In the lunch hour, I going to find out what's going on. Call him up and tell him that you are a bookbinder. You represent this and this company. And I work for you Sunday. And I'm good. You just want to find out if I'm not a thief, or beat up somebody, or fight with somebody-- my reputation, just.

speaker: interviewer
text: Because the job what I did-- you're satisfied. What they're telling you? It comes 12:15. The uncle called. After the lunch, they're going in an office. Five minutes later, they call me. Did you work with somebody else? Why? No, we just ask. To tell you the truth, yeah. Why? I cannot make a living here. What do you mean you cannot make a living here? You got almost $100 a week. Still, I cannot make a living. What do you want?

speaker: interviewee
text: I want $160. I'm going to be here. You starting 4 o'clock in the morning, I going to start 4 o'clock in the morning. You finish 9:00, 10 o'clock in the night, I going to finish 9:00, 10 o'clock in the night. If I don't have any transportation, you drive me home. But I am going to stay as long as you. What I want to have-- the week is up, I want to have $160. OK, we let you know. I go back, and I work. It comes Friday. He gives me $160. I worked up approximately another six, seven years. And what's more, he pays Uncle Sam tax. And Uncle Sam gives me every year $300 or $400 back-- $350, $360. Because he overpaid. Because my wife was working. And she took a deduction. And I took a deduction.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were working at the book bindery. She was still working at Hart Schaffner Marx.

speaker: interviewee
text: She quit at Hart Schaffner.

speaker: interviewer
text: So after that, at 10 years, they closed it. They retired. They closed it. So I was looking for a job. I found a job at Hirsh Company, in Skokie. What kind of company is that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Shelving. Shelving-- they supply Sears, everything with Sears. And I work over the 10 years. And over there, I got my first heart attack.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK. I think we're going to stop on this tape now. So you had a heart attack.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I had my first heart attack by Hirsh. And they took me to the hospital. And I was four weeks in hospital. And I returned to work. And they gave me easier job. But I got a second heart attack. And when I come to the doctor, the doctor didn't tell me nothing. He just wrote a note to the factory. When I come to work, they throw me out. I got the angina. And the angina-- they never know. You are good now, and a minute later you can be, he said, the heart attack again. So they make it a angiogram. You know what the angiogram is. And they want to make an-- I cannot have an operation. Because they don't have where to hook it up. I don't know if you heard of Dr. Rosenberg, Dr. Clark. My son is a doctor. And he works with them, over there in Presbyterian St. Luke's.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you had the best care?

speaker: interviewee
text: That's what I said, the best care. But there is nowhere to hook it up. The veins, what they're taking off on legs. There's no where to hook it up. He said to me, you can live 100 years, if you're going to take care of yourself.

speaker: interviewer
text: So are you taking care of yourself?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's already 20 years.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's good. But you've got another problem, too.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, now I've got the lung cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: So how are they treating the lung cancer?

speaker: interviewee
text: I took 32 radiations.

speaker: interviewer
text: And do they feel that you're better now?

speaker: interviewee
text: I'm on month-to-month see the doctor, and the x-rays. I lost 10 pounds. In the last two months, I didn't lost nothing. So the doctor is very satisfied. And I'm going to see him in the 20th. And if the x-ray doesn't show nothing special, and if I weigh the same thing, he say I going to be almost cured.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you feel optimistic about this?

speaker: interviewee
text: I feel OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: Good. That's good.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he ask me about my appetite. And I say, I'm always-- when you give me food, I eat.

speaker: interviewer
text: Food and cigarettes.

speaker: interviewee
text: Right. I cannot stay away.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. Well, we're almost at the end of the tape now. Is there any last thing you want to say? Nothing?

speaker: interviewee
text: No.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK, so let's stop the tape. And we'll show some of your photographs.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my grandfather, Moishe Feigenblatt. He's my mother's father. He is about 72 when he was killed by the Germans. He fight with them. They want to transport him away from the city. And he doesn't want to go. So he fight with them, until they put seven bullets in him. And he was killed. And this is my grandmother, Kayla Feigenblatt. She was 70 years old. She dies in 1943. I think she got cancer or [NON-ENGLISH]. They would call this [NON-ENGLISH]. That is cancer.

speaker: interviewer
text: That's cancer.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is my family. This is my father, my mother, myself in the right side, my brother, with a cousin and my other brother in the left side. The grave is from my grandmother. This is my second grandmother, or step grandmother, you could say. Because I got a stepmother. That's a step grandmother. She dies in 1932. And there were 10 children. And nine living left for Brazil. After the war, I wrote to them that I am the only alive one. They're asking what I want. And the only what I wanted is I told them, I want to have a Frigidaire. And I was in Israel. And they send me a Frigidaire. And I don't think anybody is alive. Because when I was in this age, maybe nine or 10, the youngest of them was 28 years old. So I don't think somebody is alive on them. And that is the grave that they put it on in Czestochowa, for the step grandmother. OK, this picture is made in '45, in Fernwald. I'm in the left side. And after then I've got two friends. We work in the hospital. And we have a lunch hour. So after then, some guy passed by. So you say, make a picture, make a picture. So this is the story with us.

speaker: interviewer
text: Go ahead.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, this is the Czestochowa book, which we put it in for memories. And that's the way I put in the ad-- some mistakes over there. But it's better from nothing. This is the story.

speaker: interviewer
text: The bedroom.

speaker: interviewer
text: In the other room.
